coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot (CQD)-modified Bi3O4Br nanosheets possessing excellent photocatalytic activity under simulated sunlight
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiameng
Zhang, Haitao
Zhao, Jianquan
Jiang, Dongmei
Zhan, Qingfeng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi3O4Br (CQDs/Bi3O4Br) nanosheets were successfully synthesized through a mild hydrothermal method for the first time. Compared with traditional photocatalysts Degussa P25 and g-C3N4, the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composites exhibited better photocatalytic activity in degrading rhodamine B (RhB) under simulated sunlight, and the degradation rate of the composites could be increased by up to 4 times compared with the pure Bi3O4Br. In addition, the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composites also presented higher photocatalytic properties in degrading tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA). The activity enhancement was attributed to the heterojunction structure formed by Bi3O4Br and CQDs, which could effectively separate photogenerated carriers. Two major active species, holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), were found in the process of photocatalysis. This work may provide a novel method for the environmentally friendly preparation of photocatalysts to remedy freshwater pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
CQDs/Bi3O4Br Composites
Photocatalysis
Photodegradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra and (c) Raman spectra of 0-B and 0.25-B.
(a) The TEM image of 0-B; (b) The HRTEM and SAED images of 0-B; (c) The TEM image of 0.25-B and the HRTEM image of CQDs; and (d) The elemental mapping patterns of 0.25-B sample.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curve of 0-B and 0.25-B.
XPS spectra of (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) Br 3d and (e) C 1s for 0.25-B.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and band gap, (b) photoluminescence spectra, (c) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves and (d) transient photocurrent density of 0-B and 0.25-B.
(a) Photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples for the degradation of RhB under simulated sunlight irradiation and (b) kinetic curves of photodegradation of RhB over various photocatalysts.
(a, c) Photocatalytic activities of the 0-B and 0.5-B samples for the degradation of TC and BPA under simulated sunlight irradiation and (b, d) their corresponding reaction kinetics curves.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation efficiency of (a) 0-B and (b) 0.25-B to RhB with various scavengers; (c) A proposed enhancing photocatalytic mechanism over CQDs/Bi3O4Br heterojunction under simulated sunlight irradiation.
(a) Recycling tests for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over 0.25-B under simulated sunlight irradiation and (b) their corresponding reaction kinetics curves.
The synthesis process of the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite.
The quantum yield of 0-B and 0.25-B samples.
The pseudo first order rate constants k’ and the corresponding residual values R2 of Fig. 6. (b).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot (CQD)-modified Bi3O4Br nanosheets possessing excellent photocatalytic activity under simulated sunlight

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument, School of Physics and Electronic Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument
School of Physics and Electronic Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument, School of Physics and Electronic Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi3O4Br (CQDs/Bi3O4Br) nanosheets were successfully synthesized through a mild hydrothermal method for the first time. Compared with traditional photocatalysts Degussa P25 and g-C3N4, the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composites exhibited better photocatalytic activity in degrading rhodamine B (RhB) under simulated sunlight, and the degradation rate of the composites could be increased by up to 4 times compared with the pure Bi3O4Br. In addition, the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composites also presented higher photocatalytic properties in degrading tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA). The activity enhancement was attributed to the heterojunction structure formed by Bi3O4Br and CQDs, which could effectively separate photogenerated carriers. Two major active species, holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), were found in the process of photocatalysis. This work may provide a novel method for the environmentally friendly preparation of photocatalysts to remedy freshwater pollution.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of industry, the huge consumption of fossil energy not only leads to energy shortages, but the products of combustion also bring about a series of environmental problems. In addition, water pollution caused by the wanton discharge of industrial sewage and accidental oil spills also threaten the survival and development of aquatic animals and even humans.

Photocatalysis, as a possible low energy input technology, can be used for water splitting to produce hydrogen and degrade harmful pollutants in aqueous solutions, which has alleviated the environmental pollution to a certain extent, and provided a new way for the development of new energy sources [1–3]. Compared with classic photocatalysts such as TiO2 and ZnO, bismuth-based semiconductors have been widely used in the field of photocatalysis due to their smaller band gap and more efficient photogenerated carrier mobility [4–8].

BiOBr, for its excellent optical and electronic properties as well as unique [Bi2O2]2+ layered structure, has attracted increasingly researchers' attention among various bismuth-based semiconductors. Particularly, the higher ratio of Bi to Br in Bi3O4Br lead to a more negative potential of CB during the debromination of BiOBr, thereby affecting the energy band structure and enhancing the reduction ability of the photocatalyst [9,10]. In order to further improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi3O4Br, recombination with other semiconductors to form a heterojunction is one of the common modification methods. Guo et al. and Liu et al. decorated Bi3O4Br with α-Bi2O3 and BiOCl, respectively. The decrease of α-Bi2O3 hole position in the composite leads to stronger oxidizability, and the heterojunction structure formed by BiOCl modification increases the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [11,12]. Yu et al. synthesized the Ag/AgBr/Bi3O4Br composite by ion exchange method, the photo-generated electron-hole pairs were effectively separated and showed better photocatalytic performance through the synergetic effect of Ag nanoparticles between AgBr and Bi3O4Br [13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new pattern of nanomaterials with a diameter of 2–10 nm [14], possess amorphous nanocrystalline cores and principally consists of graphitic turbostratic carbon (sp2carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridised carbon insertions, which shows superior electron transfer and reservoir ability. In addition, the excellent photoelectric features [15] and outstanding up-conversion photoluminescence properties [16] of CQDs promote the transfer of photogenerated electrons and enhance the light utilization [17–19]. Therefore, a series of CQDs-based composites with high-efficiency photocatalytic properties were synthesized such as CQDs/BiOBr [20], CdS/CQDs/g-C3N4 [21], TiO2/BiOI/CQDs [22], etc. Based on the unique physical and chemical properties of Bi3O4Br and CQDs, we anticipated that the heterojunction formed by these two semiconductors could show a strong photocatalytic capability.

In this paper, we synthetized the CQDs/Bi3O4Br heterojunction photocatalyst for the first time by a mild hydrothermal method. The bismuth bromide oxide was debrominated by adjusting the pH value of the solution, and the CQDs were recombined on the surface of Bi3O4Br through the intermolecular force. Compared to Bi3O4Br under simulated sunlight, the composite exhibited better photocatalytic performance in degradation of rhodamine B, tetracycline hydrochloride and bisphenol A, which results from the improvement of the photogenerated carrier separation efficiency of the system. In addition, the recycling tests also showed the great stability of the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), Ammonium oxalate monohydrate (C2H10N2O5),ethanol(C2H6O),glucose (C12H12O6) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and iso-Propyl alcohol(C3H8O) were bought from Shanghai Runjie Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Tetranitro Blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) was bought from damas-beta (China). All the other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The CQDs were synthesized by improving the typical alkali-assisted method reported elsewhere [19]. 100 mL of deionized water was used as solvents for 10 g C6H12O6 and 4 g NaOH, and then the solution was ultrasonicated for 12 h. Subsequently, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 using HCl (1 M), and was further dialyzed using dialysis membrane with retained molecular weight 1000 Da for two days to remove impurities.

1 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 1 g CTAB and 0.25 mL CQDs solution were dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water. NaOH solution (1 M) was added to the obtained suspension to set the pH of the solution at 11.9, followed by continuous stirring at room temperature for 60 min. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 12 h. The products were collected and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol to remove residual ions, and was finally dried by vacuum for 8 h. The synthesized samples were labelled as 0.25-B. Similar procedures were adopted in the preparation of 0-B, 0.125-B, 0.50-B, 0.75-B and 1.0-B, where 0, 0.125, 0.25, 0.75, and 1.0 represented the volume of the CQDs solution. The synthesis process of the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The phase structure of the samples was analyzed by XRD (RINT-2000) with Cu Kα radiation. The FTIR spectroscopy was performed on a Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700 to obtain the data ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Raman spectra were obtained using an invia-reflex micro-Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) with a 532 nm wavelength incident laser. A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope was utilized to acquire the TEM images. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to determine the chemical bonding environment. With the help of a UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Japan Shimadzu Corporation, the UV–Vis DRS was recorded. Moreover, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method (Micromeritics TriStar II 3020) was applied at 77 K to determine the specific surface areas based on N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. A Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (excitation source: 380 nm) was used to examine the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum. Quantum yield (QY) and time-resolved spectra measurements of fluorescence decay were conducted using a FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Furthermore, the transient photocurrent response was measured in a three-electrode system, and Na2SO4 (1 M) was chosen as the electrolyte. (CHI630E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.).

The photocatalytic properties of samples were evaluated by the degradation of RhB (1.0 × 10−5 mol L−1), TC (10 mg L−1), and BPA (1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1). A Xenon lamp (500 W) was used as the simulated sunlight source in the photodegradation process. 20 mg, 50 mg and 50 mg of the photocatalyst were respectively dispersed in 50 mL of the aqueous solutions of RhB, TC, and BPA by ultrasonically bath followed by vigorously stirring for 1 h in a dark environment so as to reach the adsorption-desorption balance. The concentration of the suspension was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600) after being centrifuged.

The phase structure of 0.25-B and 0-B were investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the peaks located at 2θ = 23.9°, 28.2°, 29.0°, 31.4°, 35.5° in XRD patterns of 0.25-B and 0-B are well indexed to the (112), (015), (114), (200) and (211) planes of orthorhombic Bi3O4Br (JCPDS # 84–0793). Furthermore, 36 mg CQDs were present in each 50 mL carbon point solution after freeze-drying, and the weight ratio in 0.25-B after being combined with Bi3O4Br was less than 0.1%. Thus, no characteristic peaks of carbon can be detected in 0.25-B sample owing to the tiny amount of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite [23].

In addition, the molecular structure of the photocatalysts was identified by the FTIR spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. 1b, The 534 cm−1 absorption band appears in both 0-B and 0.25-B samples corresponding to the stretching mode of Bi–O [24], and the bending vibrations of the O–H groups of the adsorbed water molecules trigger the stretching vibration peaks at 3427 and 1637 cm−1 [25]. Particularly, it can be seen from the inset of Fig. 1b that 0.25-B has a peak at 1386 cm−1 caused by the stretching vibration of the C–O group compared with pure Bi3O4Br [26], which indicates the interaction between CQDs and Bi3O4Br in the composite. Moreover, the Raman spectra of the 0-B and 0.25-B samples are shown in Fig. 1c. Two peaks at ~112.5 and ~159.8 cm−1 are ascribed to the A1g internal, and Eg internal of Bi–Br stretching mode, respectively [11]. The peaks position of the 0.25-B sample slightly shift towards the low Raman shift, which can be ascribed to the strong interaction between CQDs and Bi3O4Br [27].

The morphological and microstructural characteristics of the as-synthesized samples were confirmed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the nanosheets Bi3O4Br structure can be seen. Moreover, as shown in HR-TEM image of Fig. 2b, the lattice fringes of 0.286 nm is attributed to the (020) planes of Bi3O4Br and the nanosheets show good crystallinity from the SAED image in the inset of Fig. 2b. After Bi3O4Br was decorated by CQDs, the morphology of the sample did not show significant changes, but some dark dots with a 5 nm average size were attached onto the nanosheets as can be seen in Fig. 2c, and its lattice fringes of 0.204 nm was assigned to the (010) planes of the graphitic carbon in the inset of Fig. 2c, which indicated that the CQDs were successfully recombined on Bi3O4Br and the formation of heterojunction structure can effectively separate the carriers in the process of photocatalysis. Moreover, the uniform distribution of Bi, O, Br and C species in the 0.25-B sample confirmed the formation of the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite in the elemental mapping images of Fig. 2d.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the adsorption-desorption isotherms of nitrogen were used for characterizing the specific surface area (SBET) of 0-B and 0.25-B samples at 77 K. The SBET of 0-B and 0.25-B samples are 10.184 and 10.713 m2 g−1 respectively, which suggests that the decoration of CQDs has negligible effect on SBET.

XPS analysis was employed to study the chemical composition and surface chemical states of 0.25-B. Fig. 4 a shows the survey XPS spectra of 0.25-B, which is composed of Bi, O, and Br elements, indicating that the recombination of CQDs did not introduce other impurities into the Bi3O4Br nanosheets. In the high-resolution Bi 4f region (Fig. 4b), the two peaks at 164.6 and 159.3 eV are attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, revealing a trivalent state for Bi O bonding, and the other two peaks at 165.3 and 159.9 eV shows different coordination environments of Bi in Bi3O4Br [28,29]. The O 1s of Bi3O4Br in Fig. 4c can be fitted by two peaks at 530.0and 531.2 eV, which are assigned to lattice oxygen and O H bonds on the surface, respectively. In the Br 3 d region (Fig. 4d), the binding energy peaks deriving from the Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 states at 68.8 eV and 69.8 eV on Bi3O4Br [29]. The C 1s signals at 284.8, 286.0 and 289.3 eV in Fig. 4e can be assigned to C sp2-hybridizedcarbon of CQDs, C O and C O bond, respectively [30,31]. Consistent with the FTIR, Raman, HRTEM analyses, the XPS results proved the coexistence of CQDs and Bi3O4Br in the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite.

The optical properties of the 0-B and 0.25-B were explored by the UV–Vis DRS. As presented in Fig. 5 a, pure Bi3O4Br has an absorption edge wavelength of around 463 nm. However, The 0.25-B sample modified with CQDs did not show significant changes in light absorption, and in the inset of Fig. 5a, its band gap was slightly reduced to 2.63 eV, calculated by equation αhν = A (hν − Eg) n/2 , where α, ν, K and E
g represent the absorption coefficient, light frequency, constant and band gap energy respectively. Herein, the value of n equals 4 for the indirect transition of Bi3O4Br.

The photoluminescence (PL), time-resolved spectra of fluorescence decay and photocurrent response analysis were employed to study the carrier migration and separation efficiency of 0-B and 0.25-B samples. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the characteristic peak of 0-B and 0.25-B samples appeared at 470 nm, and the 0.25-B sample exhibited a significantly reduced photoluminescence emission. In Fig. 5c and d, the intensity-average lifetimes (τ) of 0-B and 0.25-B samples were calculated to be 0.3278 and 0.5103 ns respectively, and the 0.25-B sample presented higher photocurrent intensity in the test of transient photocurrent responses. These results prove that the CQDs/Bi3O4Br heterojunction structure effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers, and has higher carrier separation and migration efficiency, which may help to exhibit better photocatalytic performance. In addition, quantum yield (QY) was measured according to Rayleigh scattering principle. It can be seen in Table 1 that the quantum yield of 0.25-B sample (4.14%) is higher than that of 0-B sample (3.21%), which indicates that 0.25-B sample has a better fluorescence property [32].

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared were evaluated by degrading RhB under simulated sunlight irradiation. Compared with pure Bi3O4Br and typical photocatalysts Degussa P25 and g-C3N4, CQDs/Bi3O4Br nanosheets presented more efficient photocatalytic activity in Fig. 6a. In addition, RhB was completely degraded over 0.25-B after 18 min of simulated sunlight irradiation, exhibiting the highest photocatalytic performance among these CQDs-based photocatalysts. It is worth noting that the photocatalytic performance of the composites did not continuously enhanced with increasing the content of CQDs, which could be ascribed to the reason that excessive CQDs on the surface of the composite would hinder the absorption of Bi3O4Br [20,24]. The degradation rate constants k’ of these samples for degrading RhB were obtained by the pseudo first order kinetics model in Fig. 6b, and it could be found from the specific values listed in Table 2 that the photocatalytic reaction rate of the 0.25-B sample was about 4 times higher than that of the pure Bi3O4Br sample.

Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA), two colorless toxic organic compounds were also used to further evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the samples. As displayed in Fig. 7 a and c, the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite also exhibited higher photocatalytic properties in degrading two colorless pollutants. Moreover, the photocatalytic reaction rate of composites for TC and BPA degradation were respectively increased by 2 times and 2.5 times in Fig. 7b and d. The improvement of CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite photocatalytic performance may be attributed to the formation of surface heterojunction, which promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers and accelerates their migration.

The trapping experiment is one of the common methods to explore the main free radicals in the process of photocatalytic reaction. Different scavengers (1 mM), NBT for •O2 −, AO for h+, and IPA for ·OH, were added to the system to remove the corresponding free radicals. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, h+·and •O2 − played more important roles in the degradation of RhB regardless of the presence of CQDs. However, it is worth noting that after trapping h+ and •O2 − in the system, the sample decorated by CQDs degraded less RhB, indicating that the composite can produce more h+·and •O2 −than the pure Bi3O4Br in the process of photocatalysis, which can promote the degradation of pollutants.

According to the above results, as presented in Fig. 8c, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for CQDs/Bi3O4Br is proposed. CQDs/Bi3O4Br with the band gap of 2.68 eV can be excited by simulated sunlight. On the one hand, the electrons excited from the valence band to the conduction band would rapidly migrate to CQDs and further generate •O2 − with the dissolved oxygen around the photocatalyst in the aqueous solution. On the other hand, organic contaminants can also be degraded directly by the photoinduced holes on the valence band of Bi3O4Br. Therefore, the heterojunction structure formed by Bi3O4Br and CQD can effectively separate photogenerated carriers, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance.

Finally, the stability of CQDs/Bi3O4Br was evaluated by degrading RhB through the recycling tests and the results are depicted in Fig. 9 a and Fig. 9b. After three degradation cycles, 78.7% of RhB could be degraded in 20 min, whose photocatalytic reaction rate decreased slightly, but it still revealed the absence of significant structural changes and showed good stability of the composite.

In summary, CQDs/Bi3O4Br nanosheets were successfully prepared via a mild hydrothermal method for the first time. Compared with the pure Bi3O4Br, the CQDs/Bi3O4Br composite exhibited superior photocatalytic properties in degrading RhB, TC, as well as BPA. The controlled trapping experiments confirmed that h+·and •O2 − played more important roles in the degradation of organic pollutants. The activity enhancement was attributed to the heterojunction structure formed by Bi3O4Br and CQD, which could effectively separate photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, 78.7% of RhB could be degraded in 20 min after three degradation cycles, indicating the absence of significant structural changes in the composite. This work may provide a novel way to develop other environmentally friendly photocatalysts.

Xiameng Huang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Haitao Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Jianquan Zhao: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Dongmei Jiang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Project administration. Qingfeng Zhan: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11674336, 51522105 and 11374312).
